items.q: What do McGregor's Theory X and Theory Y describe?
items.correct: Two contrasting management assumptions: Theory X assumes workers dislike work and need control; Theory Y assumes workers can be self-motivated and seek responsibility.
items.misconception: Theory X is about working in a factory and Theory Y is about office work.
items.random_wrong: Theory X and Theory Y are stages of business growth.
items.why: The theories are about MANAGER assumptions, not industry or growth stages. McGregor's point is that managers' beliefs about workers shape how they manage and the outcomes they get.

items.correct: The external macro-environment: Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Legal and Environmental factors affecting a business.
items.misconception: PESTLE analyses the internal strengths and weaknesses of a business.
items.random_wrong: PESTLE stands for Profit, Earnings, Sales, Trade, Loans, Equity.
items.why: Students confuse PESTLE (external) with SWOT (internal + external). PESTLE is about the wider environment a business cannot control; SWOT mixes internal and external.

items.correct: An arrangement where a franchisor grants a franchisee the right to use their brand, products and systems in exchange for fees and a share of revenue.
items.misconception: A franchise is when a business owns multiple branches itself.
items.random_wrong: A franchise is when employees buy shares in their company.
items.why: Owned chains are not franchises. A franchise involves separate ownership — McDonald's restaurants are mostly franchised (owned by individual operators), not owned by McDonald's Corporation.

items.q: Luca Pacioli's 'Summa de Arithmetica' detailing double-entry bookkeeping dates from:
items.why: Correct answer: 1494. “1390” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's Net present value. “Payback” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Year Pacioli published 'Summa' codifying double-entry bookkeeping?
items.why: The real figure is 1494 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
